genre_refs: Wall Street Journal newspaper / torn-newspaper collage
description: A designed graphic styled to look like a torn newspaper clipping mounted on a white crumpled-paper background, portrait format. At the top is the masthead 'THE WALL STREET JOURNAL.' in its signature blackletter-adjacent serif. Below, a black serif headline reads 'The Case of the $400,000 Massachusetts Lobster Heist', with a bold deck: 'Crustaceans bound for Costco were stolen in a phishing and impersonation scam, a logistics company says'. A jagged horizontal black tear runs across the middle; inside this black band is the agency's inserted promotional message in white: 'ICE helps businesses fight back.' above the URL 'ICE.gov/RetailCrime', which is underlined in highlighter-yellow. The lower section returns to white paper with body text: 'Cargo thefts account for $15 billion to $35 billion in annual losses, according to estimates from the Department of Homeland Security. The agency launched Operation Boiling Point to address organized retail crime and cargo theft. The title seems particularly fitting for a lobster heist...' continuing with quotes about thieves targeting copper, eggs, and baby formula; the phrases 'Cargo thefts account for $15 billion to $35 billion in annual losses' and 'Operation Boiling Point' are emphasized in bold/yellow highlight. Messaging technique: borrowing the credibility of a prestige news outlet (WSJ) and a quirky, attention-grabbing 'lobster heist' hook to promote ICE's Operation Boiling Point and its retail-crime/cargo-theft mission, framing ICE as a protector of American businesses and supply chains. Target audience: businesses and the general public; intent is positive PR and program promotion.
notable_text: THE WALL STREET JOURNAL. The Case of the $400,000 Massachusetts Lobster Heist. ICE helps businesses fight back. ICE.gov/RetailCrime. Operation Boiling Point.
